title: Summer Pudding
subtitle: White bread soaked in summer berry juice, pressed overnight and turned out
excerpt: A bread-lined pudding basin filled with cooked summer berries, weighted and refrigerated overnight so the juice saturates the bread completely. Turned out, it holds its shape, deep purple outside, soft and saturated throughout. One of the great British summer desserts.
sourceNotes: Summer pudding is a British dessert that appeared in cookery writing from the late Victorian period onward, initially as a light alternative to the heavy suet puddings of the season. It uses the same bread-lining technique as the charlotte but with fresh fruit rather than cooked apple, and it is always made the day before serving.

content.text: Summer pudding requires patience and nothing else. The technique is straightforward: bread lining, fruit filling, bread lid, weight, overnight. In the morning, the bread will be saturated with purple juice and the pudding will hold its shape when turned out. The only risk is white patches of bread that the juice didn't reach, the troubleshooting section covers how to fix this.

Use a day-old white sandwich loaf with the crusts removed. Fresh bread compresses too much and gives a dense, gluey result. Day-old bread keeps some of its structure even after soaking. The fruit mix shown here, raspberries, blackcurrants, blackberries, is a good all-purpose combination; add or substitute redcurrants if you have them.

items.name: Bread
items.prepNote: day-old white sandwich bread, crusts removed

content.text: A 1-litre pudding basin. You will also need a small plate or saucer that fits inside the rim of the basin, plus a weight (a tin of beans will do) to press the pudding overnight.

Put {{blackcurrants}} of blackcurrants and {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar into a medium saucepan with 2 tablespoons of water. Cook over medium heat for 3, 4 minutes until the blackcurrants begin to burst and release their juice. Add {{raspberries}} of raspberries and {{blackberries}} of blackberries and cook for a further 2 minutes, stirring gently, the raspberries and blackberries should be just softened but still holding their shape. Taste and add more sugar if needed. Leave to cool.

Cut a round from one slice of bread to fit the base of the basin. Spoon 3, 4 tablespoons of the fruit juice over the base circle to colour it. Press the bread into the basin. Cut the remaining slices into fingers and line the sides of the basin, overlapping each piece slightly. Spoon a little more juice over the bread lining so it is coloured on the inside, this helps ensure no white patches remain on the outside when turned out.

Spoon the fruit and all its juice into the lined basin, packing it in firmly. Cover the top with more bread slices, trimmed to fit and overlapping to seal. Spoon any remaining juice over the bread lid. Place a small plate directly on top of the bread and put a heavy weight on the plate. Refrigerate overnight or for at least 8 hours.

Remove the weight and plate. Place a deep serving plate over the basin and invert. Hold for a moment, then lift away the basin. The pudding should drop out as a firm, deep-purple dome. Serve with cold double cream or crème fraîche.

items.fix: Spoon any reserved fruit juice over the white patches, they will soak in within minutes and turn purple. Always reserve a few tablespoons of juice for this purpose.
items.cause: Not enough juice reached those areas of the bread
items.symptom: White patches of bread visible after turning out

items.fix: Use day-old bread and chill for at least 8 hours. A shorter chilling time means the fruit juice hasn't had time to set the bread.
items.cause: Not chilled long enough, or bread was too fresh and went gluey

items.fix: Make sure the bread strips overlap at least 1 cm. Pack the fruit firmly but not beyond the basin rim, leave 1 cm of headroom.
items.cause: Bread gaps in the lining, or too much fruit was packed in

items.fix: Taste the cooked fruit and add sugar to balance before filling the basin. The fruit mix can also be adjusted, less blackcurrant and more raspberry will give a sweeter result.

content.text: Summer pudding turned up in British cookery writing in the late Victorian years, first as a lighter option to the heavier steamed puddings of the day. The bread-lining trick comes from the same tradition as the apple charlotte. Because nothing is cooked or baked once it is built, it suits summer well. The dish stands or falls by the fruit. A summer pudding made with good British raspberries and blackcurrants at the peak of the season is exceptional. It is one of the most seasonal British puddings, tied to a six or eight week window in July and August.
